perturbation.original_text: Cash Sales shall exclude any merchant fees, taxes, shipping, refunds (e.g., returns, right of rescission, NSF checks, and credit card chargebacks), rebates, and bad debt
perturbation.changed_text: Cash Sales shall exclude any merchant fees, taxes, shipping, rebates, and bad debt
perturbation.explanation: The removal of 'refunds (e.g., returns, right of rescission, NSF checks, and credit card chargebacks)' from the definition of 'Cash Sales' directly impacts the consumer's right to refunds, including the right of rescission, especially pertinent in sales related to education and training programs. This omission allows LEA to potentially calculate royalties on gross sales without factoring in legally required refunds, thereby violating consumer protection laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 16 CFR Part 429, Cooling-Off Period for Sales Made at Homes or at Certain Other Locations
perturbation.law_explanation: This regulation grants consumers a three-day cooling-off period to cancel certain sales made at their homes, workplaces, dormitories, or temporary locations of the seller. By excluding 'right of rescission' and 'returns' from the definition of 'Cash Sales', the contract creates a financial disincentive for LEA to honor these legally mandated refunds, potentially leading to non-compliance with the FTC's Cooling-Off Rule. This rule applies nationwide, including in California and Colorado, where the parties T&B and LEA are incorporated, respectively.

perturbation.justification: The omission of 'refunds (e.g., returns, right of rescission, NSF checks, and credit card chargebacks)' directly undermines consumer protection, specifically the right of rescission. The original text included these refunds, whereas the changed text excludes them, creating a situation where LEA might calculate royalties on gross sales without accounting for legally mandated refunds. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references Title 16 CFR Part 429, which concerns the Cooling-off Period for Sales Made at Homes or at Certain Other Locations, highlighting the legal basis for the right to cancel certain sales. By omitting 'refunds' and 'right of rescission,' the changed text contradicts the legal requirement to honor these refunds, as described in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: LEA shall be responsible for, and shall pay, all sales, value added and similar taxes, if any, which may be imposed on any receipts of the Trainings sold hereunder, as well as any other tax based upon LEA's use of the Licensed Intellectual Property in connection with the Business.
perturbation.changed_text: LEA shall be responsible for, and shall pay, all value added and similar taxes, if any, which may be imposed on any receipts of the Trainings sold hereunder, as well as any other tax based upon LEA's use of the Licensed Intellectual Property in connection with the Business.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting 'sales' from the types of taxes LEA is responsible for, this creates potential non-compliance issues in states where LEA operates and collects sales tax. While LEA is still responsible for other taxes, this creates ambiguity which could lead to legal disputes. Specifically, the omission shifts the burden of sales tax liability and collection, which contradicts the legal obligation of vendors.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Revenue and Taxation Code Section 6051
perturbation.law_explanation: This California law mandates that a sales tax is imposed on retailers for the privilege of selling tangible personal property at retail. Removing 'sales' tax from LEA's responsibilities creates ambiguity and potential violation, as the retailer (LEA) is legally obligated to collect and remit this tax. T&B Seminars, Inc. is a California corporation, so any business in California is subject to the CA Rev & Tax Code § 6051.

For the privilege of selling tangible personal property at retail a tax is hereby imposed upon all retailers at the rate of 2 1 / 2 percent of the gross receipts of any retailer from the sale of all tangible personal property sold at retail in this state on or after August 1, 1933, and to and including June 30, 1935, and at the rate of 3 percent thereafter, and at the rate of 2 1 / 2 percent on and after July 1, 1943, and to and including June 30, 1949, and at the rate of 3 percent on and after July 1, 1949, and to and including July 31, 1967, and at the rate of 4 percent on and after August 1, 1967, and to and including June 30, 1972, and at the rate of 3 3 / 4 percent on and after July 1, 1972, and to and including June 30, 1973, and at the rate of 4 3 / 4 percent on and after July 1, 1973, and to and including September 30, 1973, and at the rate of 3 3 / 4 percent on and after Octo
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'sales' from the taxes LEA is responsible for directly contradicts California Revenue and Taxation Code (RTC) 6051. This law imposes a tax on retailers for the privilege of selling tangible personal property at retail. By removing 'sales' tax, the changed text fails to explicitly assign responsibility for this legally mandated tax, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In any instance to which such indemnities pertain, LEA shall obtain and maintain necessary insurance, including, without limitation, Commercial General Liability Insurance, including product liability insurance, trademark infringement, copyright infringement, defamation, contractual liability and personal and advertising injury liability insurance in an amount no less than ten million dollars ($10,000,000.00) per occurrence and ten million dollars ($10,000,000.00) aggregate combined single limit. T&B and Tarek El Moussa shall be named as an additional insured on such insurance and proof of such inclusion shall be provided to T&B.
perturbation.changed_text: In any instance to which such indemnities pertain, LEA shall obtain and maintain necessary insurance. T&B and Tarek El Moussa shall be named as an additional insured on such insurance.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specificity regarding the type of insurance (Commercial General Liability, etc.), the required amount of coverage ($10,000,000.00), and the requirement to provide proof of insurance, the modified text creates a legally risky situation. It weakens T&B's protection against potential liabilities arising from LEA's actions and fails to comply with industry-standard insurance requirements for businesses of this nature. Omitting the minimum coverage amount especially could result in T&B being underinsured and expose them to significant financial risks. This clause also previously required annual proof of inclusion on the insurance to be provided to T&B, which this change removes, as well.
perturbation.law_explanation: This Florida statute addresses liability insurance policies and requires certain minimum coverage amounts for businesses. While not explicitly mandating a specific amount for all businesses, the statute highlights the importance of adequate coverage and proper disclosure. The removal of a specific coverage amount and details about insurance type in the contract creates a risk that LEA's insurance may not adequately cover potential liabilities, which could leave T&B exposed. Legacy Education Alliance Holdings, Inc. has a listed address in Cape Coral, FL, and is thus subject to the business liability laws and standards in the state of Florida.

F.S. 627.4147 627.4147 Medical malpractice insurance contracts. — (1) In addition to any other requirements imposed by law, each self-insurance policy as authorized under s. 627.357 or s. 624.462 or insurance policy providing coverage for claims arising out of the rendering of, or the failure to render, medical care or services, including those of the Florida Medical Malpractice Joint Underwriting Association, shall include: (a) A clause requiring the insured to cooperate fully in the review process prescribed under s. 766.106 if a notice of intent to file a claim for medical malpractice is made against the insured. (b)1. A clause clearly stating whether or not the insured has the exclusive right to veto any offer of admission of liability and for arbitration pursuant to s. 766.106 , settlement offer, or offer of judgment if the offer is within policy limits. An insurer or self-insurer shall not make or conclude, without the permission of the insured, any offer of admission of liability and for arbitration pursuant to s. 766.106 , settlement offer, or offer of judgment, if such offer is outside the policy limits. However, any offer for admission of liability and for arbitration made under s. 766.106 , settlement offer, or offer of judgment made by an insurer or self-insurer shall be made in good faith and in the best interest of the insured. 2. If the policy contains a clause stating the insured does not have the exclusive right to veto any offer or admission of liability and for arbitration made pursuant to s. 766.106 , settlement offer, or offer of judgment, the insurer or self-insurer shall provide to the insured or the insured’s legal representative by certified mail, return receipt requested, a copy of the final offer of admission of liability and for arbitration made pursuant to s. 766.106 , settlement offer, or offer of judgment and at the same time such offer is provided to the claimant. A copy of any final agreement reached between the insurer and claimant shall also be
perturbation.justification: The original contract text specifies the type and amount of insurance coverage required, as well as proof of coverage, offering a defined level of protection. The changed text omits these specifics, weakening the protection for T&B against potential liabilities. While the provided Florida Statute 627.4147 focuses on medical malpractice insurance and doesn't mandate specific coverage amounts for general business liability, it underscores the importance of insurance policies and clauses related to liability. The omission of specific coverage details and proof of insurance requirements creates a potentially legally risky situation where LEA's insurance might not adequately cover T&B, contradicting the intent of the original clause to provide clear and enforceable insurance protection. The change undermines the original explicit protections, creating a significant risk of underinsurance.
